AI Regional Healthcare Coordination Platform

Connecting the Right Patient to the Right Hospital with the Right Information

Shizheng Wang · Marc Hosana · Gayeong Lee · Haley Yahiku (MS Data Analytics)  |  Shanghai Jiao Tong University · Ajou University · UNLV

Project poster — problem framing, three-country comparison, AI methodology, and choropleth results.

01 · Project breakdownWhat this project is

South Korea's healthcare problem is not a shortage of resources — it is misallocation. Korea became a super-aged society in 2025 (65+ = 20.3% of the population), yet patients bypass regional hospitals for Seoul-area tertiary centers, roughly half of the ~9.38 million annual ED visits are low-acuity (KTAS 4–5), and patient records sit trapped in siloed EMR systems. This project proposes a single AI coordination layer that fixes the three failure points at once, and — the part documented on this page — quantifies the regional-equity problem with a reproducible Healthcare Accessibility Index built independently for South Korea, China, and the United States.

From structural pressures to the platform's three pillars (structure follows the project outline).

The patient journey the platform enables

Why compare three countries?

Each system fails differently, and each failure motivates a platform module. Korea has unrestricted patient choice, so demand concentrates in Seoul. China pursued top-down digital triage (AI in WeChat/Alipay, 18,000+ medical consortia) — proof the navigation-and-referral mechanism works at scale. The US invested heavily in EHR interoperability (HITECH Act, 21st Century Cures Act, HL7 FHIR) yet data remains fragmented across proprietary platforms. Running the identical accessibility pipeline on all three shows the misallocation pattern is structural, not uniquely Korean — and benchmarks where the platform's equity gains would be largest.

02 · The data analysisThe Healthcare Accessibility Index

The core analytical deliverable is a 0–1 Healthcare Accessibility Index computed for every region of each country — 17 Korean sido, 31 Chinese provincial units, 50 US states — under two conditions: the current state from official statistics, and a projected state under the AI coordination platform. Both are rendered as choropleth maps on a shared color scale (the maps on the poster's right column).

The design constraint driving every method choice: every input must be citable to an official source, and every assumption must be visible and stress-tested. The pipeline deliberately uses simple, transparent methods over complex ones, because the argument is about allocation, not prediction.

03 · MethodologyThe seven steps — process and reasoning

1

Data assembly — two indicators, fully referenced

Each region gets exactly two inputs: doctors_per_1k (physician supply) and pct_65plus (elderly share, the demand proxy). Keeping the input set minimal keeps every number citable:

CountryPhysician supplyElderly shareIndependent cross-check
Korea (17 sido)KOSIS e-Regional Indicators, doctors per 1,000 at medical institutions, 2023Statistics Korea 2025 Senior Statistics + MOIS resident registration (national 20.3%)MOHW National Assembly data (2022): Seoul 3.37/1k highest vs. Sejong 1.23 lowest — same ordering
China (31 units)China Health Statistics Yearbook 2022 (licensed physicians per 1,000, 2021)7th National Population Census, Bulletin No. 5, Table 5-2 (exact)2023 national bulletin (3.40/1k national) + press rankings of oldest provinces
USA (50 states)AAMC Physician Workforce Dashboard, active physicians per 100k, 2022 (÷100)US Census Vintage 2020 estimatesAAMC 2024: Massachusetts highest, Idaho/Mississippi lowest — same ordering
Data honesty flags. Where a value could not be text-verified, it is flagged rather than hidden. Korea: 8 regions carry aged_est = True (65+ share interpolated from MOIS capital/non-capital aggregates). China: 24 of 31 doctors_per_1k values carry doc_est = True (transcribed from commonly reproduced yearbook tables, constrained by 7 text-verified anchors — Beijing 5.1 max, Jiangxi/Guangdong 2.5 min). USA: all values exact, no flags. Step 7 tests whether conclusions survive perturbation of every flagged value.
Why exclude Washington, DC? DC has 925.7 physicians per 100k — 1.9× Massachusetts — because it is a city-scale jurisdiction hosting a multi-state metro's workforce. Including it would compress all 50 states into the bottom quarter of the scale. It is drawn in neutral grey, exactly how the China notebook treats Hong Kong and Macau (different statistical systems).
2

Need adjustment — the "per capita effect"

Raw physician density ignores who the population is: 1,000 residents of Jeonnam (27.4% elderly) generate far more care demand than 1,000 residents of Sejong (11.7%). Each region's supply is deflated by its relative elderly burden:

Si  =  doctorsiagedi / agednational

A region with the national age structure keeps its raw density (denominator = 1). Worked examples from Korea: Jeonnam 2.6 → 1.93 effective (burden ratio 1.35); Sejong 2.1 → 3.64 (ratio 0.58 — young city, low per-capita need).

Why this form? It is the simplest member of the need-adjusted supply ratio family used in health-workforce research: one interpretable ratio, no fitted parameters, every input citable. A regression or gravity model would add precision the source data cannot support — and would obscure the argument.
3

Normalization — min-max to a 0–1 index

Indexi  =  Si − SminSmax − Smin
Why min-max instead of z-scores? A bounded 0–1 range matches the benchmark framing and reads intuitively on a color scale (0 = worst in the country, 1 = best). Z-scores are unbounded and assume a distributional shape the 17–50 region samples don't justify.
Interpretation caveat. The index is relative within one country, within one year. Korea's 0 (Gyeongbuk) and the USA's 0 (Idaho) are not comparable levels of care, and 0 does not mean "no access."
4

Platform scenario — proportional gap-closure

Indexproji  =  Indexi + γ (1 − Indexi),    γ = 0.30

The platform adds no physicians. It raises effective accessibility through coordination: AI triage removes non-urgent demand from scarce ER/tertiary capacity, capacity-aware routing reallocates patients across regional facilities, and telemedicine substitutes remote care for low-acuity cases (KTAS-4 in Korea, Level-4 预检分诊 in China, ESI-4/5 in the US) where supply is thin.

Why proportional to the gap? Because uplift scales with (1 − Index), the most underserved regions improve most — Gyeongbuk 0.00 → 0.30, Idaho 0.00 → 0.30, Chongqing 0.00 → 0.30 — while each country's best region stays at 1.00. The platform's regional-equity mechanism is expressed in one line of algebra, and the maps read as "red regions turn orange/yellow; green stays green."
Academic honesty. γ = 0.30 is a scenario assumption, not an estimate — consistent with the project's "modelled estimates; prospective validation required" framing. Step 7 re-runs the pipeline at γ = 0.15 / 0.30 / 0.45, and the interactive chart below lets you drag γ yourself.
5

Map geometry — SVG boundaries parsed into matplotlib

Boundary shapes come from the MIT-licensed @svg-maps npm packages (south-korea, china, usa). Each <path> is parsed with svgpath2mpl into a matplotlib Path; the SVG id attributes match the data keys, so the join needs no lookup table.

Why not GeoPandas/shapefiles? The deliverable is a stylized poster map, not GIS analysis. SVG maps are tiny, dependency-light, and license-clean. Label anchors are polygon centroids via the shoelace formula computed on each region's largest ring, so offshore islands (Jeju's islets, Alaska's chain) don't drag labels into the sea; regions too small for in-place labels get manual leader-line offsets.
6

Choropleth rendering — design decisions

Three deliberate choices, held constant across all three countries:

Shared color scale Both panels use Normalize(0,1) + RdYlGn (red = poor, green = good), so improvement between "current" and "projected" reads as pure color intensification — no rescaling tricks.
Values printed on regions The index number is printed on every region, so the maps survive grayscale printing and casual screenshots.
Geometry integrity SVG's y-axis points down, so y-limits are inverted; aspect("equal") prevents distortion. No-data units (DC, Hong Kong, Macau) are drawn grey.

7

Verification, sensitivity, export

Invariant checks (asserted in code): index bounds are exactly [0, 1]; projected ≥ current everywhere, with the largest gains in the reddest regions; and rankings match the independent cross-checks from Step 1 (Korea: 2022 MOHW ordering; China: press rankings of oldest provinces + verified supply minima; USA: AAMC 2024 extremes and HRSA rural shortage areas).

Sensitivity 1 — the scenario parameter. The pipeline re-runs at γ = 0.15 / 0.30 / 0.45. Rankings are invariant to γ (the transform is monotone), so the choice of γ changes the size of the projected gains, never who gains most.

Sensitivity 2 — Monte Carlo on flagged inputs (500 runs):

CountryPerturbationResult
ChinaAll 24 estimated doctors_per_1k values jittered U(−0.2, +0.2)Bottom-5 set {Anhui, Chongqing, Heilongjiang, Liaoning, Sichuan} preserved in 100% of 500 runs
USAAll physician densities jittered U(−0.1, +0.1) — larger than any plausible vintage driftExact bottom-5 preserved in 15% of runs (Arkansas and Iowa are near-tied at 2.13, so the 5th slot flips) — but all five contained in the bottom-8 in 79% of runs: the Mountain-West / Deep-South underserved cluster itself is stable
Why report the 15% honestly? Because it is the right conclusion: the analysis supports "these states form the underserved cluster," not "this is the exact 5th-worst state." Reporting instability where it exists is what makes the 100% result for China credible.

Each notebook ends by exporting the full table to CSV (accessibility_index_data*.csv) for reuse.

04 · ResultsExplore the index — all three countries

Every region's current index (solid) and projected index under the platform (light), computed with the exact pipeline above. Drag the γ slider to change the gap-closure assumption — note that who benefits most never changes, only by how much.

γ = 0.30

What the three countries show

🇰🇷 Korea

A 2.3× raw supply gap (Seoul 5.0 vs. Gyeongbuk 2.2 per 1k) that widens after need adjustment, because the underserved provinces are also the oldest (Jeonnam 27.4% elderly). The aging–supply mismatch is the platform's core target.

🇨🇳 China

The bottom cluster is the aged Northeast + Sichuan basin (Chongqing, Sichuan, Anhui, Liaoning, Heilongjiang) — resource-rich cities like Shanghai still rank low after need adjustment because their populations are old. Robust in 100% of perturbed runs.

🇺🇸 USA

A stable underserved Mountain-West / Deep-South cluster (Idaho, Mississippi, Wyoming, Alabama, Arkansas) matching HRSA shortage designations — despite the US having no restriction on patient choice, the opposite of Korea's problem.

05 · ScopeLimitations & future work

The index measures relative allocation, not absolute access. Cross-country level comparisons are out of scope by construction; only the pattern of misallocation is compared.

Two indicators only. Beds, nurses, travel time, and insurance coverage are excluded to keep every input citable; the MOHW cross-check confirms beds tell the same regional story in Korea.

γ is a scenario, not a forecast. The 30% gap-closure is a modelled assumption. Future work: pilot the platform with regional health authorities and validate the triage and matching models against real-world clinical data — replacing γ with a measured effect.

Population-based, not area-based. Per the Gini/Theil literature on Chinese physician allocation (Zhang et al., 2021), population-based measures understate geographic sparsity (Tibet's high index reflects a young population, not easy physical access).

06 · SourcesKey references

  1. KOSIS (Korean Statistical Information Service), e-Regional Indicators: doctors per 1,000 population at medical institutions, 2023. kosis.kr
  2. Statistics Korea, 2025 Senior Statistics; MOIS resident registration demographics 2025 (national 65+ = 20.3%; capital 18.8% vs. 23.7% elsewhere).
  3. Ministry of Health and Welfare data via National Assembly Health & Welfare Committee (July 2022) — regional physicians/nurses/beds per 1,000. Medical World News, Oct 11 2022.
  4. National Bureau of Statistics of China, 7th National Population Census Bulletin No. 5, Table 5-2, May 2021.
  5. National Health Commission, China Health Statistics Yearbook 2022, table 2-2-2 — licensed (assistant) physicians per 1,000 by region, 2021; and 2023 Statistical Bulletin on Health Development.
  6. AAMC, U.S. Physician Workforce Data Dashboard (2022) via Becker's Hospital Review, Nov 2023; AAMC 2025 Key Findings.
  7. U.S. Census Bureau, Vintage 2020 Population Estimates — 65+ share by state, via Population Reference Bureau.
  8. Zhang et al., Equity analysis of Chinese physician allocation based on Gini coefficient and Theil index, BMC Health Services Research, 2021.
  9. Boundary geometry: @svg-maps/south-korea, @svg-maps/china, @svg-maps/usa v2.0.0 (MIT).
  10. National Emergency Medical Center (Korea), 2023 ED statistics — ~9.38M visits, ~50% KTAS 4–5; OECD (2022) on EMR interoperability.
